deficit_irrigation_greens.primary_goal: Maintain acceptable playing quality while avoiding chronically soft, saturated surfaces.
triggers: High disease pressure from chronic moisture

program.approach: Replace less than full evapotranspiration and supplement with hand watering based on observed need.
program.fit: Works best where crews can monitor and react quickly to hot spots.

cautions: Deficit irrigation without hand watering can become chronic stress.
Do not confuse wilt hot spots with disease by default.
Layering and poor distribution uniformity can make the same percentage replacement behave very differently across a green.

syringing_for_canopy_cooling.primary_goal: Reduce leaf temperature and short-term wilt during heat stress without rewetting the full rootzone.

program.approach: Apply a very light, quick water film to cool the canopy and then allow the surface to dry back.
program.best_fit: Greens under acute summer stress where full irrigation would over-wet the rootzone.

cautions: Syringing is not a substitute for correcting poor rootzone moisture or irrigation scheduling.
Repeated midday wetting can aggravate disease if the canopy stays wet.
Use the minimum water needed for cooling.

hand_watering_hot_spots.primary_goal: Target moisture where turf actually needs it instead of overwatering entire surfaces.
triggers: Dry fingers on edges, mounds, or south-facing shoulders

program.approach: Train staff to use probes and visual patterns to treat only the stressed area.
program.best_fit: Greens and high-value surfaces where precision matters more than blanket run time.

cautions: If the same areas always need water, fix distribution, hydrophobicity, or rootzone issues.
Avoid turning hand watering into constant surface puddling.
Document recurring weak zones for longer-term correction.

wetting_agent_strategy_for_lds.topic: Wetting agent strategy for localized dry spot
wetting_agent_strategy_for_lds.primary_goal: Improve infiltration and rewetting consistency on hydrophobic sand profiles.
triggers: Recurring wilt in the same footprint

program.approach: Use preventive or early-season wetting agent programs before severe LDS develops, then support with hand watering and probing.
support_practices: Needle tining or venting where sealing occurs

cautions: Wetting agents help a physical water-management problem; they are not a fungicide.
Very dry hydrophobic areas may still need repeated rewetting and hand watering.
Program selection should match soil type and irrigation style.

fairway_deep_infrequent_irrigation.primary_goal: Promote deeper rooting and more resilient fairway performance while limiting shallow, weak rooting.

program.approach: Irrigate to refill the effective rootzone, then wait until the surface and rootzone genuinely need water again.
program.fit: Most useful on fairways and roughs where firm conditions and rooting depth matter.

cautions: Heavy soils and layered profiles may not tolerate the same interval as uniform sand or loam.
Traffic compaction can limit the rooting benefit of deeper irrigation.
Distribution uniformity problems often get worse when longer intervals are attempted.

irrigation_uniformity_audit.primary_goal: Separate system-performance problems from turf or agronomic problems.
triggers: Recurring dry rings or wet pockets
The same wilt areas every day

program.approach: Audit head performance, nozzle selection, pressure, arc, spacing, and run time assumptions on representative zones.

cautions: Do not keep adjusting runtime to hide a hardware problem.
Topography, wind, and nozzle mismatch can undermine otherwise sound schedules.
Uniformity work often delivers more benefit than simply adding water.
